commentBody: I’m not one to make things with yeast due to the numerous rising failures I’ve endured. But hey— it’s a new year! So I made this yesterday. And, per usual, I could not discern if the dough had actually risen, both times. But I persevered, soaked it over night in a container, and voila! It turned out deliciously— even though I’m unsure the dough ever doubled. One more thing— it cracked open a bit at the top during baking, like bread would do.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've been making these for years... no, actually... decades..., only with water and regular flour (1tbs per 2 cups of flour).  I use butter sometimes... Earth Balance sometimes.... sometimes a mixture of the 2... sometimes milk... sometimes cream... sometimes add some sugar for shortcake... sometimes cheese for a cheesy biscuit.  They are endlessly variable.  I usually don't even let the dough rest.  I just flatten it out, cut the biscuits and bake.  They are basically foolproof.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hello from Atlanta! This is a old southern recipe and I've never heard of using raw sweet potatoes. They don't have the 'water' content of carrots. Can't imagine how that comes out. We've made this cake for generations and I'd like to share that you may want to try baking the potatoes. Baking deepens the flavor! Peel them, pulse the pulp to smooth it, then add that to the dry ingredients. Pecans will taste even better. Walnuts would be yumyum too. Add a tiny pinch of ground cloves!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Dear Lisa Donovan, 
This cake sounds great -must try it! But first, please help!
By Sweet Potatoes, I assume you mean the ones that have lighter skin and pale yellow flesh. Could what I call Yams, the ones with dark reddish skin and golden-orange flesh, be used instead? Or do you call those darker ones 'Sweet Potatoes'? Those two tubers' names get mixed up in recipe descriptors, there are many species, but my local supermarket and my brain only know those two varieties of pointy-ended potatoes.

commentBody: Anyone else ended up with a super liquid frosting? author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this into the layer cake with 1.5cu of frosting in between layers. I either mis-scaled or the frosting did not yield enough to fill the layers, make the advised crumb coat, and the final coat, so my sides ended up being a semi-“naked” cake. That said, the cake is fabulous and was devoured at Thanksgiving. The batter mix is super easy and the cake layers bake super flat so no need for trimming. Definitely course chop the toasted pecans - everyone loved the occasional crunch. Everyone thought it was a really good “carrot cake,” but I think the sweet potato component made it unique.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Having lived in France for decades, my take on this is simple: Steep 6-8 sliced garlic cloves in cream with salt, 5-bay ground pepper, and nutmeg for at least 30 minutes; stirring so it doesn’t stick.
Peel potatoes and slice paper thin. Don’t bother ‘arranging’ them in the baking dish, just layer them and don’t leave gaps.
Test the cream, it should have the same salinity as sea water.
Pour liquid over potatoes and bake until bubbling over. 
No cheese. To the French that is heresy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’ve been making the gratin dauphinois for decades and often serve it as a main course. It started with a Joël Robuchon recipe (he used cheese, fyi) and has evolved over the years as I’ve picked up “astuces” from friends, French and American, I’ve made it with. Here is some of their sage advice:
-cut the potatoes with a knife. The irregularity of widths and angles allows the liquid to seep between the slices.
- Use white pepper to avoid a speckled cream. 
-bake at 400 degrees for about an hour, but keep it covered the first 30 minutes to avoid over browning. 
- after rubbing the garlic in the dish, save the cloves and just before baking tuck one in each corner of the dish (I use 2 cloves, each cut in half).
-use crème fraîche (or a mixture of liquid cream and crème fraîche)
- if you heat the seasoned cream, there is no need to cook the potatoes in it. Just add a lot of the hot cream between each layer. It’s far less messy this way and you can arrange the potato slices more precisely. I’ve tried both ways and it makes no difference in consistency.
-for extra crustiness dot the top with butter. 
-and pure heresy: I put in a few pinches of piment d’espelette. Not enough that anyone would notice, really, but it adds a savory layer I like.

And, I peel the potatoes and blanch the potato slices in water and some milk beforehand.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I love cheese as much as anybody but I like this dish a lot better without it. 

I also like to make sure there's a little extra cream than it looks like it needs. As it begins to brown toward the end, use a large spoon or similar to push down the surface and allow the unbrowned cream to rise. Keep this up for a good 1/2 hr or so. The starchy cream will reduce and thicken, and you'll wind up with a gorgeous gratin on top.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My cousin, who was a chef in his hotel-restaurant of the Dauphiné region (where the gratin dauphinois comes from) would always, always say there is absolutely no cheese in the real gratin. The cream and nutmeg should be enough. I cross the line and add Comté, whose fruity flavor complements the nuttiness so well.
He also would pre cook the potatoes in milk infused with cloves of garlic before hand and then just lay them in a buttered dish, then lightly cover with some crème and pepper.

There are related problems with the potatoes. Modern potatoes are stored cleaned and in an atmosphere that caused their skins to age but importantly reduced their water content - and yes the French did/do peel them. This meant a dauphinoise could be made with whole milk,

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have no comment about the Franco authenticity of the recipe, but am here to report that there is way too much thyme and nutmeg in this version for my taste. I like both, but not a whole nut and 2 tablespoons of thyme leaves worth. I used some dried (a reduced amount as I know dried has a stronger flavor than fresh; I have little patience for stripping off that many leaves), but found even 2 teaspoons too much. Personally, if I make it again it’ll be with a few scrapes of nutmeg and maybe a teaspoon of dried thyme; certainly none mixed with the cheese.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Nice! An easy cast iron bake with a delightful, fluffy texture. Used a heaping cup of fresh blue (and pink) blueberries but think the batter could handle even more berries. Slightly cut the sugar to 3/4 cup which suits us but may not be sweet enough for some. The olive oil flavor is distinct, so use one you like! Lemon zest was lovely but think I’ll try orange zest next time to complement the olive oil. Mine was done in 30 minutes, on the nose. 

Glaze would definitely be next level!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I threw in 18oz (by weight) of fresh, room temperature blueberries. Perhaps 3 cups. It baked up beautifully in my well-seasoned ten inch cast iron skillet. There was no danger of overflow. Although I did sift the flour and baking powder together, I didn’t get too precious with the mixing procedure. The wet ingredients can be mixed all together in one bowl. Then just dump in the flour/baking powder/salt. Mix partially and gently, then add the berries. Barely mix! Needs 3/4 tsp table salt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this for my morning coffee group yesterday and thought it was fabulous . After a disastrous attempt at making the Peach Ricotta Cake (I got too enthusiastic with the amount of peaches I put in and it never got beyond mushy in the center). I stayed close to the recipe and instead of dumping more fruit in the cake I made as compote of peaches and blueberries so those who wanted more fruit could simply spoon it onto their piece. it was delicious, and because I lowered the sugar by a quarter cup, the fruit was the man attraction.  I could easily see making this for a dinner dessert by making a more exotic fruit compote and perhaps adding whipped cream. I will definitely make this again.
P.S. I baked it an extra 5 minutes per other comments.

commentBody:
So easy, so good!

I don't bake much but wanted to surprise my wife, and I've enjoyed the richness of olive oil cakes. This did not disappoint. There's apparently a good deal of chatter about 1 c. of sugar being too much: as someone who does not like an overly sweet dessert and does not often partake of desserts at all, this had just the right balance. 

My conundrum was whether to glaze or not to glaze. I decided to gild the lily, but cut the amount of the glaze by half, which was plenty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Didn't want to use my cast iron pan and no 10" cake pan, so used a 9" cake pan plus a silicone muffin pan. Worked! 

But... next time I'll put more batter in the cake pan and use only half or so of the muffin cups instead of all 6 (filling the empties with plain water) because the cake came out too shallow.

I should've greased & floured the silicone cups too or else waited longer for cooling; they stuck somewhat, leaving remnants in the pan (which of course had to be taste tested!).

Next time. And there will be a next time - really delicious!

commentBody: Pretty much a disaster. Cooking time needs to be MUCH more than 35 minutes. I took it out after top was browned as prescribed. Waited for it to cool. Glazed it. It takes almost that long just to grate all that lemon zest. A big waste of a lot of good ingredients.

commentBody: This recipe modded really easily for the GF and dairy-free people in my house. I just subbed GF all-purpose flour (Namaste brand) for the regular flour, and I used the creamiest oat milk I could find in place of the whole milk (Planet Oat barista-style, for those keeping track...). author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made in a standard 9-cup Bundt pan, using a flour/butter spray and it worked out very well! Let it cool 10-15 minutes before unmolding. 

Also used 2 cups of blueberries, floured, and they didn't sink at all. 

Didn't have enough lemon juice, zest, so added orange. Tastes great! 

No buttermilk so used yogurt and milk, which is always in my fridge.

IOW this is a very tasty, and flexible, recipe. Don't be afraid to experiment, substitute, as need be.

commentBody: This recipe is a keeper, written just as is. Those commenters wishing to add more berries would be doing so at their peril : the recipe already results in a texture that is moist without being soggy, and I think adding any more liquid to the batter would be a mistake. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
For a few years now I have asked (as have others) for high altitude modifications on each recipe. Apparently, to no avail; despite getting emails in return that basically say - “we are working on it and it will be available soon”. Many of us are reticent to try your baking recipes; thinking that we would be wasting ingredients - especially at a time when  eggs cost nearly $1 per egg!

Come on folks, even store bought cake mixes do this. Many of us live at 5000 ft. or higher and really need help making such modifications on baking.

recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody: A caution for folks who want to adapt this recipe to other fruits: tart apples and plums are both high in pectin, which is essential to avoid having your pie turn into a big, messy, squishy fruit soup dumpling. That's also the reason for using a thin layer of filling. Most other fruits will need significant thickening and/or the structure of a pan, though if you froze some rhubarb in the spring, it's a fine sub.

recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is just like the quick dessert my mother made us in my childhood when there were too many apple “seconds” (slightly bruised or damaged) leftover to sell from our apple orchard. 
The best part was - it really was a hand held slice of apple pie, thinner and less juicy, we could have more than one slice at a time. Great to eat anytime, like a cookie.

recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody: I made this for a dinner party and it was delicious. However, I had to fiddle with the recipe a little. Where I live (Midwest), none of the apple varieties called for in the recipe are available in August (when the recipe was originally published). I solved the problem by substituting half Granny Smith apples and half Gala. Also, the amount of apples (2 pounds) was far too much. Next time I will use 1.5 pounds at most.

recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody:
Finally pulled this recipe into use - late winter so no plums, and took the admonition on cinnamon to heart - added some tapioca flour and almond flour to the fruit (old apples) along with some cardamom and amaretto in place of Armagnac.
Took more like 60 minutes to cook, but came out well served with whipped cream- everyone at the dinner party loved it!
Thanks

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
If your problem is excess zucchini, the solution is this brilliant three-ingredient Spanish soup, astoundingly good for the effort:
1) Thin-slice half as much onion as zucchini. (If you're confronting doorstop marrows on the zucchini front, scoop out the spongy center first.)
2) Heat a goodly amount of olive oil in a pot and cook the onions until tender and translucent.
3) Add the zucchini and proceed likewise.
4) Take a stick blender to it, taste for salt, and serve with a lashing of olive oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious! I heavily salted the zucchini and fennel and let them sit in a colander for 20 minutes to remove some moisture. (Did not rinse, they’d end up too soggy. Despite the heavy salting, they did not taste overly salty when done draining.) 

Had a bag of sugar snap peas in the fridge I added—cut off the tips then sliced on the bias. Added some nice substance to the meal without disrupting the light and fresh vibe. 

Skipped the mint as I didn’t have any at hand, though I bet it would be a delicious addition. 

Added a light drizzle of some good olive oil and some extra black pepper before serving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The slicer opening on the side of my box grater worked perfectly for this! Was trying to use up farmers' market leftovers so subbed thinly sliced cucumber and julienned carrots for the fennel. I feared the cucumber would make salad leftovers slimy, and I reserved all nuts to mix in with each serving, but it was delicious day-of and as lunch for 2 days following. If you end up with very watery salad you could def puree into an improv salsa!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: So I'm going to try this, because pistachios--yes! But my favorite method is to take normal rice krispy treat mixture, spread out half in a buttered dish, sprinkle it with some chopped dark chocolate and place the dish in a 200 degree oven so the chocolate softens. Then bring the dish to a counter and use an offset spatula to spread the chocolate. Then press the remaining rice krispy mixture on top of the chocolate (using wet hands helps). Let cool completely. It's like an inside out candy bar.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve made these twice now and they’re a go to for me. 

Amendments:
Salted butter
Salted pistachios, double or even triple the amount
Double sesame seeds
All tahini instead of pistachio butter (in THIS economy!?)

THE BEST THING TO EVER HAPPEN: I bought condensed milk and came home and realized it was coffee condensed milk, Bandi foods brand. It tastes so incredible with this recipe and adds a little bite or earthiness that balances all the sugar so beautifully, I can’t recommend it enough.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Any RKT is likely delish and never needs to last more than a day or two.  And I adore pistachios.  But I found it unnecessarily complicated for the result.  The sesame seeds were a nice easy add, and I already brown butter for a lot of baking.  But the pistachio cream is a very mild taste compared with just doubling the amount of pistachios, the sweetened condensed milk makes it crazy sticky to blend and put in pan, and despite the current fad for vanilla bean paste it’s not better than good quality vanilla extract (and the paste has other junk in it). I do like halvah but it’s already so crazy sweet halvah is not necessary.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Tip: Pistachio butter is easy to make. Roast 1 cup of pistachios for 8 minutes at 325F. Transfer to a food processor (+ a pinch of salt!) and blend for 10–15 minutes (scraping down the sides every so often) until smooth and creamy. (It will go from crumbly to doughy to creamy, I promise!) This will make more than 2 tablespoons. Cover leftovers and store in the fridge.

commentBody:
Just finished making these.  Used the ingredients in the quantities listed, but put all the pistachios and halvah into the rice krispies mixture instead reserving some for a topping. author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
*Dynamite* recipe. Made it several times. Guests and friends are crazy about it. 
However, having laughed at other commenters who insist on messing with any and all NYT recipes, I've found that:
* It's OK to skip the pistachio butter/cream ($$$!!). Hard to source where I live and no difference discernible. Works fine to use 4T tahini total & no pistachio butter.
* The condensed milk is overly sweet and gooey here (IMHO). It's great without that cloying, over-the-top sweetness.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Objectively delicious. 

For those wondering about pistachio butter: If you have a small capacity food processor, take a handful of pistachios, grind/blend till it almost starts to seize up. Add a smidge of coconut oil and a splash of vanilla to bring it to a "butter" consistency (& avoid the death of your food processor's motor). Works a treat. I can also vouch for pre-mixing (and ever so slightly +ing) all additions aside from salt.

recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Made as written with the exception of the halvah which I excluded due to availability. I found unsweetened pistachio butter at my farmers market. The result was divine and a hit in my office. I didn't find them to be too sweet - the bitterness of the tahini was well balanced by the sweetness of the condensed milk (also adds texture). Don't skip on the roasted sesames or pistachios, they add a nice nuttiness and texture that really ups the ante. 10/10 would and will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I love the concept and combined tahini, pistachio & halvah flavors. However, sadly this recipe did not work for me. My family is vegetarian and I could only find large vegetarian marshmallows. Somehow they melted lumpy instead of smooth and loose. The result was a dense mixture which did not coat the krispies uniformly. My family commented that they were ok tasting but on the salty side. Also, a pet peeve of mine are recipes that call for small amounts of different ingredients which adds up in cost and leaves a lot of opened unused leftovers. All in all, Not worth it. 

Now, what to do with a 2/3 full can of sweetened condensed milk?

commentBody: I made these and while they are tasty -- browned butter adds a nice caramel flavor -- they are too sweet. I used condensed unsweetened coconut milk, so that is not the cause of the excess sweetness. I also used jarred marshmallow fluff instead of marshmallows because the ingredient list for marshmallows is appalling. And the ingredients for the jarred fluff were not actually too bad. However both the fluff and the Rice Krispies are sort of naturally sweet, and together they are too much.

commentBody:
I had to make these vegan for my vegan family members and it was still really good! Used Mykonos vegan butter, Trader Joe’s marshmallows and sweetened condensed oat milk (Nature’s Charm brand that I found at Whole Foods). For the pistachio butter I used the food processor and made it myself. No other ingredients needed. (Next time I’ll make extra pistachio butter because it was so good!)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: A note for cooks using pistachio cream instead of the specified pistachio butter.  Pistachio cream is a sweeter, silkier more dessert-y element typically has some vegetable oil and sweetener mixed in with the ground pistachio paste.  If you're using pistachio cream and then think this is too sweet, it could be that ingredient is causing it to be so.  Pistachio butter is typically ground pistachios and salt.  Also, your source matters.  Fiddyment Farms (pistachio grower CA) is a good one.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love French lentils and use them in salads like this one frequently. The veggies used change with the seasons:  spring asparagus barely cooked and garlic chives. In summer tomatoes juicy and bright in color and flavor. In winter, I’ve been known to add sweet potatoes or  butternut squash. Prosecco vinegar is an elegant choice with good olive oil, but plain old lemon juice works too. And herbs, dried or fresh tie it together. So folk, have fun, go to Paris or Rome or some beach in faraway Asia for a culinary adventure. Use the joy of this salad to enhance your everyday life.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This looks good and similar to lentil salads I've made, but it seems too many herbs here. Rosemary, thyme, bay leaf - fine, parsley, fine. Then you really start competing here when adding in the strong taste of radicchio, AND tarragon, AND dill. I think you have to pick and choose the herbs you use or risk a salad tasting a bit confused. I'd go with the first 3, or go with the tarragon and omit the top 3, or go dill and aromatics. I feel like it's an American tendency to think more is more.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
We've made a salad nearly identical to this for the past 25 yrs when a friend introduced it to us at her home on St. Barths. The only difference- the addition of tarragon vinegar and lardons (we now use diced pancetta). She cooked the lardons slowly in olive oil to render as much fat as possible, scooped out the crisp bits, and then made the dressing (  tarragon vinegar) in the skillet in which she'd prepared the lardons as the lentils cooked. She then combined it all after draining the lentils. 
Over the years, we've topped it with crumbled goat cheese, stuffed it into fat heirloom tomatoes, and eaten it directly from the storage bowl - refrigerator door still open. It's that good.

commentBody: This was fantastic. Checks all the boxes: healthy, wildly adaptable, and great for leftovers. I most definitely did not line the lentils on a towel to cool. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A pound of lentils surely needs a tablespoon of kosher salt. But whisking it into roughly half a cup of oil, vinegar and lemon juice (a challenge in and of itself) yields a sharp, salty result. Not bad, just means you need other dishes, fish or other protein, to complement and distract. Next time I’ll add the salt to the lentils when they are 10 minutes shy of being done and make this traditional French vinaigrette in the traditional way: with salt and pepper to taste, understanding that lentils may need more than a green salad. Use the herbs in any combination that suits your personal taste, but certainly keep the quantity of fresh parsley which could even stand alone. Love the combination of root carrot, chicory radicchio and generous amount of shallots. This is a versatile keeper, although I’m not quite sure how to rate it because of the salt treatment.

commentBody:
It's a cold, rainy day here in Australia and this recipe has brightened my day up no end. 
The last of the tarragon from the garden as well as some parsley went in along with a home grown lemon (so, so many lemons this year).
It's so zesty and zingy it has to be good for you. 
Can't wait to make it in summer,  when everything (except my lemons) are in season. 
Also,  reckon it would go great with fennel... might try that next week.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Fun fact about raddichio - unless they are exposed to frost and periods of cold, along w periods of darkness, they’ll remain incredibly bitter.  I don’t buy chicories from CA for this very reason.  Chicories from the Pacific Northwest however are much more mild and delicious.  We grow them in northern MN.  Deer won’t touch them until the frost comes, then they disappear!

(Love this salad!  I sub red cabbage when I can’t find a good raddichio.)

Sorry, this recipe didn't do it for me.  I'm a big fan of lentils, especially the Frenchy ones, but this tasted like eating grass.  
For those interested, Ina Garten does a nice lentil salad that is similar but without all (or most) of the green stuff.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Honey isn't used in real French vinaigrettes but if you need your dressing sweet… Here in France, I cook my lentils with bouquet garni (purchased pre-assembled) and a few cloves stuck in a piece of carrot for easy removal, as well as chopped carrot if I have the time. Make sure not to overcook lentils so that they turn mushy; 15 minutes after the boil is my magic number. I agree with another commenter here, drain after cooking, rinse once, dress and serve warm!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious! My radicchio was mild but it would still work had it been more bitter. I diced the carrots and air fried them instead of leaving raw. I also boiled the lentils in salted water with dried herbs mixed into the water, and decreased the salt in the dressing to two teaspoons to compensate for the salted water. Finally, I used two small red onions instead of shallots.

Some feta and toasted walnuts would not go amiss in this salad. So many possibilities!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: WAY too much salt in the vinaigrette. Despite the ratings on this one, I strongly believe this must be a typo - 1 tablespoon should instead be no more than 1 teaspoon. You can always add more later to adjust, but that full tablespoon was burning my mouth, as well as wilting the colors and volume of the vegetables much too soon before serving. Other than that, this is a good recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First time made it as is. Great flavor. Dressing is perfect. It's a beautifully looking salad if you serve it right away. (On day 2, the lentils were soggy, radicchio lost its color, and the salad did not look appealing.) Second time, I tweaked the quantities to my liking (cut the shallots to 3-4oz and parsley to 3oz, and upped the carrots to 10oz) and served after two hours. Added radicchio before serving. Had some candy cane beets and used them on top as garnish for extra cheer.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad is SO good and also made us very... bloaty. ;) The recipe doesn't suggest it, but I highly, HIGHLY recommend soaking the lentils in advance as I now know that will help remove much of the oligosaccharide sugar that causes the gas. Also wouldn't recommend eating this as a main dish! I'm hoping soaking will make these more digestable because this is so so yummy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I soaked the radicchio in ice water for about 10 minutes to help cut the bitterness, and used all the fresh herbs except for dill, which I just didn’t have. Didn’t miss it…The salad was crunchy, flavorful and delicious. Mine turned out very peppery, probably because I used finely ground pepper instead of fresh ground pepper. Next time I’ll go lighter on the pepper but otherwise wouldn’t change a thing. Thank you for this delicious, healthy recipe!

On the plus side, it’s a healthy and hearty salad, just not for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe was good but not great (many NYT recipes could use a few more rounds of resting). The lemon was overpowering — next time I’ll do a vinaigrette (w balsamic or red wine vinegar). The lentils were bland — I will cook them w salt next time. The raw carrots were weird. I would sautee them a little. I would add chunks of feta too (I used to buy a lentil salad from a deli that did this). Text time. Some people love this recipe but I will heavily revise it if I make it next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Although green lentils don’t strictly need overnight soaking, it makes them easier to digest. They contain phytic acid and oligosaccharides (raffinose-family carbs), which can cause bloating and reduce mineral absorption. Soaking helps break these down before cooking.
Even if you cook lentils until soft, those compounds aren’t fully removed by heat alone. Soaking also helps them cook more evenly and a bit faster.
That said, if you tolerate lentils well, you can skip soaking and just cook them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Two years ago I enlisted my family in making legumes a bigger part of our diet, but lentils have largely been relegated to soups. For this I used French lentils, skipped the dill (just didn't feel like trying to track it down) and went a little heavier on the tarragon (which is simply magical in this dish). I also used "mild" treviso radicchio since it was available at same cost. My 15 year old son declared he'd "eat the (love) outta (this salad)."  Even better on day two!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I kept the basic proportions but modified the ingredients slightly because of what was on hand. Dried thyme instead of fresh. No fresh tarragon. Hot honey. I didn't have radicchio but used a comparable amount of chopped Belgian endive. After slicing the carrots very thin (at an angle) I par-cooked them in boiling water for about a minute and a half then plunged them into ice water. They were still crisp but the texture was a little more compatible with the whole creation. I agree with others who said but this is a flexible recipe. Great flavors and textures. I will make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great for weekday lunches. Used French green lentils, cooked until just starting to get tender. Added walnuts to make complete vege protein. Skipped the lentil drying out part & just patted dry in the colander then added to the bowl. I put all the lemon juice & rind in at once, & added thinly sliced shallots only to taste to avoid overpowering the salad. Salt to taste, not 1 tbsp. Only 1 tsp honey. Couldn't find radicchio so sub'd arugula (keep separate until ready to eat). Herbs are perfect.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This looks like a good recipe and similar to on I make without measuring anything but eyeballing it. I think one can simplify the shopping and preparing of some of the ingredients by substituting dry herbs:thyme, rosemary, dill and tarragon. It may be that expending the extra effort of going to the store and procuring the fresh herbs may produce a more flavorful dish, the savings in time and effort suggests it is not worth it. I also question how important it is to cool the lentils on a pan....

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad was delicious. The dressing was excellent. Next time I make it, however, I will not rinse the lentils with cold water. It made too much of a mess spreading them on kitchen towels, for one. Number two, if you double the recipe, like I did, you end up, needing a lot more space to spread out the lentils. I didn’t have that space so the lentils never really dried and as a result, the dressing was watered down. It was still incredible though and will make this again and take my chances  With not rinsing the lentils in cold water.

commentBody: I love recipes that leave room to improvise.  I didn't add lemon zest (used it in ravioli filling as I was meal prepping).  Didn't add dill as I don't like it. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Excellent.
 
Lesson 1 - While I used fresh dill and parsley, I used dried thyme, rosemary and tarragon.   The tarragon wasn't an issue, but the dried thyme and rosemary are sticks in the dressing.  So I put the dressing with the dried herbs in the Magic Bullet and that worked beautifully.  
Lesson 2 - Next time I make this I'm going to leave the dressing in the Bullet and whiz the parsley and dill into it too before combining the dressing with the carrots and shallots to save time.

I like tarragon, and, over time, the tarragon blends with the other flavors.  I don’t think the radicchio is necessary.  That is your call.  As one reviewer noted, I think this prep would benefit from the addition of lardons or bacon.  Again, your call.  I may make this again.  I’d like to try the basic idea with beans rather than lentils, just to trey something different.

commentBody:
So so so good!

Made as is with a few exceptions….

- added roasted Japanese sweet potatoes into the whole dish. 

It’s going into the monthly rotation for sure!

commentBody: This dressing is an awesome base for a French-inspired lentil salad. I didn’t have radicchio or shallots but I did have beet greens, fennel and red onion. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I'm obsessed, even with rushing! I put rosemary and thyme directly in the pot with some Rancho Gordo green lentils...used an almost empty dijon jar and eyeballed some agave in place of honey and shook it up...this allowed for a bit of extra vinaigrette to be poured over once served. Did not have fresh tarragon.
Next time, will follow to the letter for the sake of comparison, but once I tasted the vinaigrette, I knew it was going to be good... I ate it for dinner AND lunch--wish I'd made more!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve now made this twice in the last month, modifying slightly each time and each time it was wonderful.  I used green French lentils, adding some pearl couscous the first time bc it was in the fridge and needed to be used.  My carrots were less than ideal so I roasted them with the shallots along with some fennel that also needed to be used.  We loved it with the roasted veggies and will likely keep making it that way.  As for the herbs, I only used the bay leaves to make the lentils and put a few pinches of dried thyme in the dressing.  Added copious amounts of dill and parsley chopped fine.  Salt and pepper to taste; dried mustard powder bc my mustard was too old.  oh and the second time I didn’t have radicchio so subbed arugula.  Was a wonderful light dinner in this brutal heat wave.  I get that I changed things around; however as with many NYT Cooking recipes it was easy enough to do that with a good road map. And for me reading the notes is key—folks out there are excellent cooks and have helped my cooking over the years.

commentBody: This version of the cake is unfamiliar to this Southerner, but it's an excellent recipe.  The Swiss buttercream is far better than the usually over-unctuous American buttercream.  I still prefer a lighter frosting for coconut cake.  For those hesitant to use the sugar, choose another cake.  Coconut cake is traditionally very sweet, and it fades if it's not supported adequately by sugar.  That said, the 2 cups of sugar can be reduced by about 1/3 cup without doing real damage.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My southern mother made a coconut cake every Christmas. I remember my dad grating fresh coconut for what seemed like forever. (I don’t think there was ever a cake free from his dna) Mother iced with old fashioned boiled icing, which required the cake to need stabilization with small dowels. Her family recipe has been passed down, and continues to be made for Christmas dinner. Not always beautiful, but always delicious. (And thanks to modern day shredding tools, no dna!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just this morning happened to make a coconut cake for the first time, from the Women's Home Companion Cook Book, dated 1944 -- my go-to for cakes.  A much simpler recipe than the one herein and quite delicious.  But the cakes were flat.  I see in the photo accompanying this recipe that the cakes are also fairly compressed (despite the egg white), and I am going to chalk that up to the moisture in the coconut in the batter.  It's good to have this coincidental reference.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In Atlanta, a dinner featuring the multi-layered coconut cake from Rich's department store bakery was about as fancy as you could get without being at an actual wedding.  Long after the store was bought by another chain, the Atlanta newspaper ran the Rich's recipe - but there was an error (gasp!).  The following week, the paper printed the corrected recipe.  It was a big deal, and I still have both those clippings in my recipe box.  Someday I might be ambitious enough to give it a try.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: With the whisk attachment, whip the buttercream on medium/high speed until it is white, fluffy, and doesn't taste buttery, this can take 8-10 minutes. To counteract the yellow in your buttercream, add a drop or two of violet food color gel to make it whiter. Then switch to a paddle attachment and mix it on low speed for 15-20 minutes to make the buttercream very smooth and remove air bubbles. This isn't required, but if you want really creamy frosting, you do not want to skip it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake came out great! The coconut flavor is there but not too strong (you will not feel like you’re eating tanning lotion!). And I liked the very slightly boozy flavor of the frosting. The cake is all butter and so is a bit dense—you’ll want a small piece. If you don’t know what to do with all the leftover egg yolks, you can instead use pasteurized egg whites from a carton. Despite what you may have heard, they will whip into meringue—just add some cream of tartar to help stabilize them. I just did it to make this cake and frosting yesterday, and it worked fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is my first NYT Cooking note! I have been using this for years, and I sometimes enjoy the comments more than the recipes. I made this for Easter. The 11 leftover yolks felt like a decadent discard in the age of Avian flu. The cake is delicious, tender, and, yes, sweet. One of my layers was a bit underdone due to eyeballing the division of batter in each pan. The point is this: three layers is a lot of cake, particularly when the cake is this rich. My two-layered cake is better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe makes a DELICIOUS cake. I followed the instructions to the letter, EXCEPT I slowed the mixer to the lowest setting when adding the powdered sugar to the frosting (so it didn’t dust up all over the kitchen). I then mixed about 1 cup coconut into the frosting. The frosting was still quite runny, so following the Tip, I put the frosting back to the mixer on a higher speed, about 7. It firmed up nicely within seconds. During final assembly, I found that I didn’t really need another 3 cups of coconut for the finishing touches. I toasted about 1 cup coconut for sprinkling on the top. It was yummy and a big hit with my guests.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a gluten free version. It took about 4 hours plus the time to frost it the next day. I could not find gluten-free cake flour so I followed a substitution that I found online where you replace two Tbsp of flour per cup with 2 Tbsp of cornstarch. I also replaced 3/4 cup of flour with 3/4 cup of coconut flour. I followed the King Arthur suggestion of letting the batter sit for 20 minutes before baking (for GF batter). I did that right after I mixed the liquid and dry ingredients with the butter. I left out the leavening at that point. After the 20 minutes, I added in the baking soda and baking powder and then folded in the egg whites. I had to bake it just over 30 minutes. It only rose to the the top of the pan. It was also gummy to touch. Since the icing was runny, I put everything in the refrigerator overnight and that gave the cake a chance to dry out a bit. However, the icing broke and I could not restore it. The texture sort of looked like shredded coconut so I just went with it. It would not stick to the sides of the cake, so the cake had naked sides. This turned out to be one of best tasting cakes I have ever made in my life! Everybody absolutely loved it and nobody knew any better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This was delicious! I only had unsweetened coconut, do I used that both in cake and mixed into the frosting. As others said, it was already pretty sweet. But for the portion applied to the outside, I toasted half, then tossed it with about 1/4 cup confectioners sugar before pressing onto the cake- before that, it was a bit bland.

The cake was HUGE though, could have served 12-16! Next time, I’ll reduce the volume and make just two 8” layers.
In the meantime, I’m wondering if I can freeze a portion of the cake, possibly pre sliced. Does Swiss butter freeze well?

commentBody: We made it....and loved it. Yes, I too clutched my pearls at the butter, sugar, all the things. We had smallish slices and devoured every crumb, and will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are a lot of walk down memory lane comments in here that have nothing to do with this recipe, which isn't helpful for a girl baking a coconut cake for the first time for her mom's 71st birthday. So here are my tips for THIS recipe: the sponge was incredible! I did cut the sugar by half a cup. Definitely use coconut extract, it's worth that extra flavor saturation in the cake part. I made it in two layers, not three. And I did take a helpful tip from one of the commenters deep in about making a 5 minute frosting: half a cup of heavy cream whipped, half a cup of sour cream, confectioners sugar and dried coconut. I forgot the extract in the frosting and it was fine. I'd double the frosting recipe for even two cakes and maybe 2.5 it for three cakes. But the frosting flavor (even frosted thinly) was delicious and I received a lot of compliments. Mostly, happily, my mother was content. Thanks for the great recipe! I'll make it again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
this is a fully gluten (and sugar free) version i’ve been making for years. letting the cornmeal hydrate over night make a huge difference. 

combine: 
45 grams (1/3 cup) cornmeal with 1/2 teaspoon each: kosher salt, baking soda, baking powder. set aside.

combine: 	
85 grams (2/3 cup)cornmeal, 1 egg , 200 grams (7 ounces) plain full fat greek yogurt (or buttermilk). refrigerate overnight.

preheat oven to 450-degrees and melt 4 tablespoons butter, vegetable oil, or bacon fat in an 8-inch cast iron skillet. combine dry and wet ingredients, add melted fat. pour batter into hot skillet. lower oven to 425-degrees and bake for 20 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My Memaw called Appalachian style cornbread “a poor girl’s milkshake”. And she always tossed in a tablespoon of fresh churned butter into the cast iron skillet before placing in her wood fired oven. And sometimes after hog killing, she added in rendered hog fat called “cracklings”. Now that’s about as Southern as you can get!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Get good cornmeal—stone-ground, if possible. Makes a big difference!
I agree with others re: heating the oil (melting the fat). Put it in the skillet and heat it in the oven at the same time.
But I have never understood putting sugar or any other sweetener in the cornbread itself. This recipe only calls for 2 tablespoons, so it’s probably barely noticeable. But I’ve had corn muffins that might as well have been cupcakes, they were so sweet!  Do yourself a favor and try it without sugar!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sugar can intensify the flavor of corn. Sugar also holds moisture so it can help keep the cornbread from drying out so easily, much like sugar in enriched breads. The amount in this recipe is hardly enough to impart a sweetness to the bread. I'd try it both ways and simply compare. Much like salt is used in breads for structure, color, and to regulate the function of yeast, you don't always taste it.

Also, I make a similar recipe that uses sour cream instead of buttermilk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Never thought I would see the day that skillet cornbread was featured in the NYT.

Cornbread and milk (as described in the tip at the bottom) truly is a staple of life in Appalachia.  My grandfather used to eat it almost every day.  It's what we called "poverty food" that could always be made with the ingredients at hand (milk and cornmeal) even during hard times, and filling enough for a long day of work. Another suggestion: Serve with apple butter (also a poverty food).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone here remember the late Lee Bailey, a food/lifestyle writer who preceded the current tsunami of such writers by a few years? He too was from Louisiana, and his trick for getting cornbread with the maximum of crispy crust was to pour very thin layers into *two* preheated cast iron skillets and proceed from there. Works for me. And although I'm a member of the no-sugar camp, I'm not going to start a war over it. Kudos to Mr. Johnson for all his good works.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You sure do complicate things. Both my graduate school educated, Southern parents made skillet cornbread regularly from the fifties until they died at 89 and 95 and used bacon grease,  no sweetener.  One bowl. No need to turn on the stove. You melt the bacon grease in the oven in the skillet you're heating. That skillet will be HOT when you pour in the batter and the hot grease will brown the crust. Why complicate something so simple?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am surprised that no one has mentioned the baking powder-baking soda issue.  I have always made cornbread from my Alabama granny's recipe. She always used soda (also no sugar), and her contention was that soda makes a more moist, and, somewhat paradoxically, lighter, cornbread.  I have tried both, and I prefer the soda.  I believe this is also why so many of these recipes use some kind of acid like buttermilk which is necessary for the soda to perform its leavening qualities.

commentBody: I place the skillet in the oven while it preheats. Then I bring it out and put the oil in. That is hot enough for me. This yields a much nicer crust but I am okay with the payoff from my easier method.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wanted to make this vegan and gluten-free so that everyone in my family could eat it. Instead of eggs, I used 4 tablespoons water plus 2 tablespoons ground flax seed (mix and let stand about 5 min.). Instead of buttermilk, used soy milk plus 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar (also mix and let stand about 5 min.). And for the flour, used gluten-free rice-based flour. It turned out so good! It had a delicious, substantial crust on the outside, moist and good "crumb" on the inside. Yum.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Buttermilk substitute: Add one tablespoon of white vinegar per cup of whole milk and let rest for 10 minutes. I used half and half because we don't use milk. I feel that using the white vinegar yields more of a buttermilk like flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother’s family made cornbread very much like this, though with sugar (jam or honey) ON, not IN. We offer tips for a good crust: When pouring hot oil from the skillet into the batter, leave a bit in the skillet. After stirring oil into the batter, sprinkle a half tablespoon of cornmeal into the hot skillet, swirl, then add the batter. Second, when taking the cooked cornbread out of the oven, flip it onto a plate and slide it back into the skillet. Return to the oven for another 4 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @DCNative  "Steaming" means exactly that, i.e., steam is rising up from the milk.  If they wanted bubbles, there are several ways to describe bubbles in cooking, because there are several ways/sizes to achieve them.  "Steaming" is a basic condition in cooking whether the substance is water or milk or juice or, 60 years ago, Dr. Pepper heated in a pan with thinly sliced lemons tossed in right as DP steams & is removed from heat.  But also, see "steaming hot," which could be beef roast or a cook.

Slowly heat the cream, then add a few ladles of it to the egg so it doesn't scamble, then add that back to the cream. Use a thermometer to slowly cook the cream/egg mixture to 178° then pour it through a strainer onto the chopped chocolate.  I put the chopped chocolate into a large flexible rubber measuring container so it's easy to pour.  It's very delicious and you can use the egg whites for breakfast.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this today with Ghirardelli’s 100% unsweetened cocoa baking bar.  Because that’s what you get when you send your spouse to the grocery store with instructions to buy nothing less than 70%.
I added an extra tbs of sugar to make up for it.  And used 20 gms of semi-sweet chocolate chips    as a substitute for 20 gms of unsweetened chocolate.  The rest was exactly as the recipe indicated.  It was sweet enough!  But it was much less a pudding/custard and more of a dense ganache texture.  Delicious, but much too rich.  We could only split one serving.  I will keep this in mind as a dessert to serve in very small portions, such as double-shot size.  Or try again with a 70% cocoa bar to see if it makes that much difference…doubtful though.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is in no way remotely similar to an authentic green pozole. Authentic green pozole has only pork meat and hominy corn in it. Once made you add chiles onion radish and lettuce. The broth is made with pork meat and pipián (a green paste made from green pepitas) 
I have no doubt that this must be delicious but it is misleading to call it pozole.

commentBody: We really liked this. We didn’t find it sour and while it was tangy it wasn’t overly acidic. I wonder if people are using under-ripe tomatillos. February isn’t exactly the best tiime of year for tomatillos and I had to pick through the selection at the grocery store to find good ones. I made it according to the recipe but subbed feta for queso fresco and creme fraiche for crema because that’s what was in the house.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very disappointed in this recipe. As some others have said, it was so bitter & sour that it was  inedible when made as written. Maybe the tomatillos were unripe, but my sense is that 2 pounds is just too much. The roasted lime also imparted bitterness, I think, and its juice overpowered the other flavors. I managed to salvage the posole with more broth and seasoning, and since I didn't have more hominy, I added corn. It's edible now, but still very sour. Proceed with caution if you make this!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It took me two days to make this delicious dish, and I agree it’s the thinly sliced radishes, the queso cheese, shredded cabbage and avocado that elevates this posole. Full disclosure: I don’t care for kale — soooo I roasted and shredded an organic chicken, substituting that protein for the kale. It made a delicious broth. I served the posole with chips although homemade tortillas would’ve been quite fun to make. On a cold winter’s day, this posole brings warmth and freshness to your family’s table. I served limeade with the posole. This dish was made with love for the family that I love.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Incredibly delicious! Made as written and revised after reading all the notes. No roasted lime. Instead I squeezed half a fresh lime into the food processor. Did not steam and peel the poblanos (they just went into the food processors with their cousins). Also: y'all scared me off the kale. The soup without garnishes doesn't have enough body, but I was generous with the garnishes!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I prefer more smoky, roasted flavor. It's better to roast tomatillos whole, and let the skins brown, before you blend them. This will give a deeper, smoky flavor- otherwise if you chop up the tomatillos and then roast, they just become watery. There's a lot of  hominy in this soup, which is nice, but I also love poblanos. Next time, I'll add an extra poblano, blend half of the poblanos with the rest of the roasted ingregidents , and chop the other half and leave as is in the soup, as instructed.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
MAKE THIS! For context: I’m white with Irish background and live in Canada. I have zero expertise with Mexican food! So- TAKE THIS AS YOU LIKE! ;) I only made changes to the recipe cause (1) my kids don’t like spicy (see note above re: white!) and (2) I’m in Canada and authentic Mexican ingredients aren’t as readily available. 
Changes made: I used about a quarter lb tomatillos cause that’s all I had; I didn’t use a poblano or jalapeño and subbed two green peppers (so sorry if this makes anyone who is Mexican reading this cringe! I cringed but it is what it is!); used basic curly kale instead of lacinato. I agree to give yourself closer to 2 hours. I DID find hominy at my local Safeway (shout out Burnaby Heights Safeway) and it was so delicious. Others have talked about replacing with white beans… sure but it’s not the same and I loved the texture and consistency of the hominy. I didn’t have quesa fresco so I used feta instead. Also added pickled jalapeño on top, in addition to other toppings cause they weren’t in it. IT WAS DELICIOUS AND DELIGHTFUL!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I loved this! This recipe strays from tradition, but nonetheless, the flavors were wonderful—bright, complex, earthy. To keep this plant based, I skipped the dairy and found the avocado was essential to bringing it all together. I added a can of pinto beans for extra protein and added a few cups of water at the end because my soup was incredibly thick and concentrated. I think cooking hominy from scratch would take this to another level, as the texture and flavor of canned was something to be desired. I’m wondering if adding fried corn tortilla strips or really good/fresh tortilla chips would be a quick way to impart that wonderful corn flavor that was somewhat lacking in the canned hominy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pozole verde is traditionally made with diced chicken. If you want to keep it vegetarian, I suggest adding white beans like cannellini. Also, this dish was traditionally made with dried hominy, called...pozole. Cooking it is similar to cooking dried beans, with a presoak and long cooking times. Using canned hominy is a lot faster. However, it is packed in water and gets a bit soggy. Drain it, pat dry, and toast it in a 425º oven 15 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
To answer Justine, below, I came across this under the heading of substitutes for hominy: "Polenta is very similar to hominy and can be used as a substitute in cooking. Use polenta as a breakfast or side dish.

It has a firm, creamy texture with a taste that is similar to light corn. In Italian cooking is usually used as a replacement for pasta sometimes. It is a suitable replacement in most recipes and you can always combine it with dumplings, tortillas, or if you are making a stew."

commentBody: I halved the recipe. This allowed me to use the 4qt airfryer for easier cleanup. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious! Acidic and hearty, perfect in wintertime or anytime! We all have enhancement ideas, so here are my three:
1. Other than the jalapeño garnish, nothing in the recipe is particularly spicy. To add a little kick, had a half dozen dashes of Tabasco. 
2. Add a teaspoon of roasted chicken flavor Better Than Bouillon to the simmering soup. Adds delicious (non-vegetarian) umami!
3. Include some sautéed ground chorizo in the toppings for a hearty non-vegetarian meal-in-a-bowl.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Terrific dish. A few points: 1. If you just get most of the skin off the poblanos, you're okay. If the poblanos aren't blistered you might want to put them back in by themselves after the other veg are out, though. 2. This is more of a stew than a soup - it's very thick. I like that but some people prefer brothier pozole. 3. It's already pretty acidic. If you want to save ten seconds of slicing and fifty cents, don't bother with the extra lime at the end (but do roast the lime as instructed).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made my own hominy from dried heirloom corn (Hopi Blue) and it was a huge flavor payoff.  A fragrant, toasty concentrated corn aroma and flavor. It's not technically difficult, but it is time consuming.  You just need dried corn, water, and pickling lime, and follow a recipe for nixtamal/nixtamalized corn.  I'll never go back to canned hominy, ever.  I also grilled the vegetables over a fire (because I already had the grill going) and added leftover carnitas meat to this.  Delish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why not roast all the garlic with the veggies in the beginning and save some for the soup as the veg come out of the oven? I blitzed the roasted poblanos with the rest of the salsa verde because I didn't care for larger chunks of pepper. This is a Rancho Gordo quadruple threat if you have access to their pozole, oregano indio, NM red chile powder (4x this:1x cumin), AND a bean... the RG buckeye bean and broth added flavor, depth, and nutrition. Love this as a playful base, as suggested :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I thoroughly enjoyed the story behind the recipe.    
 The cookies are good if you like a plain, not very sweet cookie with tea. The prep is way more than 10 minutes.   Between weighing, beating , chilling,  toasting seeds, rolling out, and cutting out, I  spent well over an hour before the first batch went in the oven. The recipe made 36 cookies.  
They were crispy; I used lard as the recipe suggested.  If you don’t have anise seeds I would suggest using almond or anise extract .

commentBody: As many others have noted this is not the traditional recipe. If you want traditional use the one linked to in the note on the NM state website, it’s the same as what’s in the 1978 copy of New Mexico Magazine Best of NM Kitchens cookbook that I snagged from my parents. For that recipe, if you don’t have anise seeds, you can easily substitute some anise extract (if I remember I also throw in some vanilla extract too). I’ll also use whatever plain, un-peaty whiskey I have if I don’t have brandy on hand (you want some of those caramel, vanilla flavors).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: New Mexican biscochitos do not use corn meal. Also I make the dough the day before (or several days before and refrigerate) in a ball, not sure what the advantage of rolling it out before cooking is. Then I take out portions and warm up on the counter for 20 min or so roll it out, cut the shapes, bake and then dip in cinnamon and sugar while still warm…. Doesn’t matter if you use butter/lard/shortening—the key is to whip it up with a mixer before you add the sugar.

commentBody: I grew up with Italian Christmas cookies, which are drier and less sweet— essentially made for dunking—than what a lot of people expect, so I was excited to try these. I followed the recipe exactly and they turned out perfectly. I found the dough very easy to work with. Half the recipe (stuck the other half in the freezer) made 23 decent-sized stars and half-moons. Love them, but if you find biscotti and the like “too dry,” this is not your cookie.

commentBody:
I loved the flavor and texture of these cookies.  Even better the next day.  It is important to use the fine corn meal or you’ll have corn grain left in your mouth.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I have made these cookies as hostess gifts all over the world.  They are uniformly loved.  Even the French devoured them!  My favorite recipe by far is from the Santa Fe, NM, Pink Adobe restaurant/cookbook (sadly closed/out of print).  Easiest recipe ever.  I substitute lard for butter and whip the butter/sugar/egg until really light and fluffy.

Pink Adobe/Rosalea Murphy
2 cups lard (I use butter)
1 cup sugar
1 egg yolk
1 tsp aniseed
6 cups flour
1 tsp kosher salt
2 tsp baking powder
1/2-3/4 cup water
1/2 cup sugar for topping
1/2 tsp ground cinnamon for topping
Preheat oven to 350.  Cream lard/butter, sugar and yolk together in mixer till light and fluffy.  Stir in aniseed.  Shift together, flower salt and baking powder and stir into creamed mixture. Stir in just enough water to make mixture hold together. Need until well mixed. On a flowered board, roll dough to 1/2 inch thickness. Cut into fancy shapes with cookie cutters. Mixed together the 1/2 cup of sugar and the cinnamon and sprinkle over each cookie. On an ungreased cookie sheet bake for approximately 10 minutes until nicely browned.  Enjoy!!!

commentBody: Now that I’ve had slice after slice each time  I pass by the cake plate in which it rests ( day 2 ) each bite is a revelation. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this as written, making the crust and filling the day before baking.  Work with the dough when it is cold but malleable.  I used a 9 " tart pan with removable bottom and no parchment paper.  I cut down on the salt a little bit in the filing, but no other changes.  After brushing the top with egg wash, do a decorative pattern and make a small whole in the center of the crust and a few slits in the top before baking to prevent cracking.  Came out beautiful and tasty after about 35 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this tonight. I wonder if the saltiness is due to using table salt instead of kosher salt. Used table salt and dialed it back by nearly half the kosher salt rec. Not too salty. My top definitely cracked. I think, if I make this again (which will depend on whether or not my suegra likes it), I will build a bit of room in the crust on top because the filling expanded and cracked the crust. Still lovely and rustic. The house smelled like heaven. Also, I took a generous sheet of parchment paper and crumpled it up in a tight ball before smoothing into and over the sides of the pan. This way, no butter leakage. The pattern on the top really emerged in the final 10 minutes of baking. Cute.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am very surprised the recipe calls for salted butter. There is no universal standard for the amount of salt in salted butter and it varies between brands. Recipes using unsalted butter will have a specific amount of salt to add precisely to control its effects on yeast. Maybe this recipe is forgiving enough? But generally, when using unsalted butter, add 1/4 tsp salt for every 1/2 cup of unsalted butter that you need.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Note: kosher salt brands are not the same. Diamond and Morton's flakes are different so they're not interchangeable by volume, only by weight. If a recipe calls specifically for Diamond and you use Morton's (or table), you need to use half the amount by weight. 1 Tbsp Diamond Crystal kosher = 8 gm 1 Tbsp Morton's Kosher = 16 gm https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/learn/ingredient-weight-chart https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2020/07/29/why-is-salt-important-in-yeast-bread

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This recipe shows a clafoutis with pretty skimpy fruit, unlike other Times recipes.  I (roughly) doubled the fruit and mixed peaches and nectarines, because that’s what I had. I baked slightly longer, because we like a cakey texture. Also, I used peach brandy, which gave it a nice spark.

As the notes say, you can use any fruit, and mixtures of fruit, like peach/blueberry and plum/apricot, even mango/strawberry. The same goes for the lovely Times strawberry spoon cake, and many others.

commentBody:

Went with a generous tablespoon of Grand Marnier, which was divine! 

Looking forward to breakfast tomorrow.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m sure this is delicious but this is NOT how clafoutis should look. I grew up in France where my mother and l made clafoutis every summer with sour cherries from our garden. There should be way more fruit than the picture suggests, and be more ‘flan-y’ than ‘cake-y’. Any sour fruit will be great for a clafoutis. The word, fwiw, is said to come from the Occitan ‘claffir’ which means ‘to fill’.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The clafoutis did start to spill over the sides of my deep fluted pie dish, so I quickly put a cookie sheet under.  Then it was still somewhat liquid 3/4 of the way through the cooking time and was spilling over the sides, but the top was starting to get quite brown.  So I lowered the heat and let it cook longer.  Absolutely delicious!  Our dinner guest asked for the recipe and made it two days later.  He did have the same liquidity problem.  Perhaps both of us used very juicy fruit?  I do weigh my ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this in July w/fresh, dark, ripe plums from a wild tree at the edge of my yard in Portland, OR. We had it on a sunny Sunday at noontime. The “less sugar, whole milk” advice in other comments worked for me. It was delicious, delightful & satisfying in every way. I write to note that btw/getting the ingredients together, pitting the plums, measuring & mixing, baking for an hour & cooling for an hour, the whole process takes a leisurely 2.5 to 3 hours. “Cooking time: 1.5 hrs plus cooling” is pretty optimistic for the occasional home baker, in my experience. So be sure to set aside sufficient time. Totally worth it.

commentBody: @McinDC hi! Thanks for the lovely note - you’re going to want to work on a dry, not oiled comal. While a good seasoned iron is always helpful and delicious, it’s not imperative. To keep your cast iron or comal seasoned, it’s good practice to wipe it with neutral oil and let it sit in a warm oven (my practice is that I wipe my comal with a little oil and throw it in an oven that has slightly cooled off from baking something - it’s the perfect temp and you can let it rest as the oven cools.)

commentBody: One of the very last things my husband's grandmother was excited to do- seated in her bed, supported by pillows- was to roll out some tortillas. She was in her late 90's, one of 12 children born in NM ca 1905. I remember her beautifully gnarled hands, still able to roll out the dough with her special small rolling pin, so pleased to be able to "help" my mother in law (whom she lived with till the end), with dinner.

recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: I love flour tortillas, although corn tortillas seem to be more traditionally Mexican. Flour tortillas are popular in the Baja. Whenever I travel there, I come home with about six kilos of tortillas from Tortilla de Harina Lulu in San Jose del Cabo. Theirs are super thin. Still have some in my freezer but I do have my tortilla press at the ready to give it a go should they run out before I return again. As other writers commented, lard would be traditional, or some hydrogenated oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: What memories this brings back!  I grew up on a sheep ranch in Wyoming. We hired sheepherders from northern New Mexico. During summers the herders stayed in sheep wagons which had a small wood burning cook stove.  I think I was about 9 or 10, staying with Tony in the wagon herding with him. Every morning he fired up the stove and made these directly on the iron stove top, and we ate them hot with butter and jam.  I remember it being some of the most delicious food I ever ate!  Thanks!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Never mind that the modern product called whole milk buttermilk is what is needed for this recipe. The commenter stating that whole milk buttermilk in effect is not really buttermilk is correct. Actual buttermilk is the byproduct of the milk that is churned to make butter, with fat removed and only a few tiny flecks of butter left. The butter and buttermilk were left at room temperature approximately overnight to become ‘cultured’ or soured. Buttermilk you pick up in the store now is not that.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: RE:  Buttermilk.  The key word is cultured.   To full fat milk is added a bacterial culture that thickens and sours it, like other soured dairy products, e.g, yogurt, sour cream, etc.  In New England, Kate’s whole cultured buttermilk is widely available.  By the way, if you have the bacterial culture, it is very easy to turn your whole milk into buttermilk, but doing so will lengthen the prep time of the recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this pie yesterday.  
This recipe filled my 2" deep ceramic pie plate to the rim.  It is delicious but in the future I will halve the recipe unless I am baking for a crowd.  For those who are questioning the 3 cups of sugar, keep in mind that this is essentially a double recipe - there is enough filling for 2 regular depth pies.  
I did not freeze my pie shell.  Instead I par-baked my homemade crust for about 7 minutes at 425 and followed the instructions otherwise.  It turned out fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
whole buttermilk is sort of a misnomer.  Buttermilk is the byproduct of buttermaking.  Most of the fat is taken out by the butter and real buttermilk is lowfat.
Whole buttermilk is cultured whole milk and is closer to yogurt made from whole milk than to real buttermilk.  I have added lemon to whole milk but I think the consistency of what the recipe asks for is closer to the consistency of a thinned out yogurt.  I would try 1/2 whole milk mixed with 1/2 whole milk yogurt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So easy to make your own whole milk cultured buttermilk – especially with Kate‘s or any other marked with “live cultures”.  Fill a 4 cup Pyrex measuring cup to almost full of whole milk; cover &  nuke until it reaches “the almost to the boil bubbles around the edge stage”.  About 12-13 min. Remove and cool to about 104°;  stir in approx 1/3 cup of Kate’s, cover with plastic wrap; then into unheated oven. Will thicken overnight and further after refrigeration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The quality of what's labeled buttermilk varies widely. At its best, its just cultured milk, with no salt or other adjuncts. Inferior brands add gums, acidifiers, colors, and flavors to mimic the characteristics that develop naturally from long fermentation. Choose the buttermilk with the fewest ingredients or, if you use it regularly, consider culturing your own. Substitute milk soured with lemon juice or vinegar if you must, but it won't be as good.

commentBody: Based on someone else's comment (about the sugar) that this filling is about the equivalent of 2 non-deep-dish pies, I cut the recipe in half and used a 9" regular pie dish. Turned out great, still plenty for 8 servings. The linked pie crust recipe was just the right size for a standard pie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Question about the butter! I've made this recipe twice now. First time as written (way too much lemon for my taste), and the second time with only 1 Tbsp lemon juice and about 1/4 Cup less sugar. This second time, a bunch of butter rose to the top and pooled in the middle, making me think that the middle was still liquid. I ended up baking an extra 30 minutes before I thought to actually toothpick the center to find it was done. Has anyone had this issue with the butter separating to the top?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This has been my go to for 30 years
1 stick unsalted Butter room temp
1 ¾ cup Sugar
3 Eggs
1 teas. Salt
1 teas. Vanilla extract
3 tabls. Flour
¾ cup Buttermilk
1   9 inch Unbaked Pie Shell
Preheat oven to 350 degrees F
Cream butter and sugar until there are small lumps.  Beat in eggs stop as soon as batter is smooth. Stir in: salt, vanilla, and flour, then stir in buttermilk until mixed completely. Pour into 9" unbaked pie shell and bake in a preheated 350 degree oven for 1 hour 10 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
"Whole buttermilk" is a confusing term, as all buttermilk is technically low-fat according to US labeling guidelines.  
Buttermilk is the result of churning cream into butter, with buttermilk remaining as the low-fat liquid byproduct.  It is then cultured.

Traditionally, or in the case of higher quality options nowadays, the cream would have been cultured first, with both the resulting butter and buttermilk being cultured products.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is a WILD amount of sugar. The following proportions worked beautifully to fill a 9 inch pie and actually tastes like the main ingredient:

1/2 tbs lemon zest
1/2 tsp vanilla extract (the 1 bean is way too much)
1/2 c. sugar
1/4 c. flour
1/4 tsp salt
3 eggs
1 1/2 c. cultured buttermilk

You'll be able to get through a slice without feeling sick. By the way, that pie crust should be pre-baked (375, blind bake 20 minutes, then remove weights and bake 15), then removed from tin and frozen.

commentBody: You can also make your own whole buttermilk (and get some homemade butter out of it too!) by taking heavy cream (or heavy whipping cream) putting it in a kitchen aid mixer, adding the whisk attachment and letting it go until it separates into butter solids and liquid buttermilk. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Growing up in Virginia, my two favorite homemade pies were "buttermilk pie" and "lemon chess" pie - both chess pie. As made in my family, the were definitely not deep dish pies; the custard layer was rather thin, in fact. I checked my mom's recipes and they were based on 2 or 3 eggs (with corresponding less of other ingredients). I think you could achieve something similar using this recipe to fill two regular pie shells. With such a rich filling less might be more.

commentBody: Having been a Chef in my own restaurant to nearly 2 decades and sifting through what seemed an endless mountain of incredible recipes, chess pie is one that escaped. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’d never had chess pie, but had heard of it for years, so I gave it a go this Thanksgiving.  I will admit; I cheated a bit; I used 2 tsp vanilla instead of a bean.

It was good, but… with so many other pies to choose from, I probably won’t be in a hurry to make this again.  It has a LOT of sugar, and not that flavorful.  It’s surprisingly chewy.  When I jiggled it to see if it was done, I thought it hadn’t set— but after 15 minutes more, I realized it had a giant puddle of butter in the middle.  So I sort of basted the top.

Like I said, it was fine, others liked it.  But if I’m going to make something this decadent, I’ll probably go with chocolate, banana cream, maple… something more interesting.  This is jes’ pie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe but used 1 Tablespoon vanilla extract instead of the vanilla bean.  I used powdered cultured buttermilk, which is low fat, and - to make it "whole" - substituted 1/4 cup whipping cream for the extra water when mixing up the buttermilk.  Yes (for a previous question I saw), leave the pie crust in its pan.  And it's important to set the pan on a sheet pan.  It was terrific!  Don't tell my secret, but we love it for breakfast.

commentBody: My paternal grandmother's family were all pie bakers...never cakes. One of my great-aunts used to drive to family reunions in South Dakota from Kentucky w/a trunkful of pies, & during the reunions she'd organize us into pie-baking teams. I had never heard of chess pie before those reunions...but my great-aunt's chess pie looked like a chessboard, & not just on the surface...the alternating dark & light colors went all the way to the bottom of the pie. I don't know how she did that...

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
1teaspoon coarse kosher salt, such as Morton (or use 1½ teaspoons kosher salt)

The author is probably referring to Diamond Crystal, which is saltier by volume, but since she doesn't name it, perhaps the recipe follower will use a brand that is more like Morton, and end up with 50% more saltiness than desired.

What worked for many, many generations of chefs and cookbook authors was uniform, granulated table salt. In almost all recipes, it simply makes zero difference which form of NaCl is used.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: To add to the confusion, in my prior post, I stated that Diamond Crystal is saltier (than Morton) by volume.  The opposite is true. Proves the point that specifying granulated table salt (what recipe authors used to refer to as simply "salt," obviates any confusion over differently textured kosher or other salts.  Unless you're eating the actual piece of salt (as with a finishing salt) there is no discernible taste difference when using one salt or another.

commentBody: Don't you just love it when someone changes the recipe? Here goes: I didn't want to half a can of sweetened milk leftover, so I just used one. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn't have a 9x13 cake pan, so used a 9" layer pan and poured the remaining batter into large bake-safe ramekins, and adjusted cooking time accordingly.  I found the longer the soak the better the cake. After 48 hours, the cake tasted just right - that cornmeal makes it unlikely to get soggy, so I wouldn't worry about over soaking or using too much milk mixture.  Spooning the extra milk mix over each slice for serving adds even more.  A big hit with my family.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake following the recipe very carefully. First of all it baked unevenly (a little higher on one side) so it was hard to pour the soak evenly at first. It was easier once the cake cooled a bit. The first 2 cups of soak went into the cake but after that they just sat on top. I stopped after 3 cups. I let the cake sit overnight as instructed. I was so excited to try it but it was dense, dry and grainy. Any thoughts as to where I went wrong or has anyone else had a similar outcome?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ok, after making this twice, I think, first, that the remarks that the cake is gritty due to the cornmeal is a function of mixing the batter. You really have to mix the batter exactly as stated in the recipe - in two 6-7 minute cycles. You really must be vigorous with it. Secondly, the comments that there is too much sauce are spot on. There is way too much sauce. I recommend making a pro rata reduction by about 1/3 of each of the ingredients.

commentBody: One thing I do for strawberry frosting is to pulse freeze dried strawberries (the kind you buy at Trader Joe’s) in my mini food processor (you could also do this by hand easily enough), then sift out the seeds directly into the icing bowl. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: LOVED this recipe. Followed comments and stuck with 1 3/4c sugar in the batter and 3c in the icing. The cake was plenty sweet but not overpowering - still a light, slightly tart strawberry taste! Decided to make my own preserves for the first time and learned some things - strawberries are high in water and low in pectin, so I ended up using gelatin to set the preserves - which in effect made this a (delish) Jello cake.
