I’m looking for a way to use up a lot of leftover jam.  This looks interesting but way way WAY too sweet. Surely you don’t need so that jam AND all that sugar…

Still it’s an interesting idea to substitute jam for part of the sugar in a recipe and REDUCE the amount of sugar.  4 cups icing sugar AND jam in the icing— holy sugar rush, gives me a headache just thinking about it. 

Fresh strawberries are an obvious improvement, but bc then I’m still stuck with all that jam!!  What to do??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made another version of this cake using fresh strawberries - note that they are full of water so cooking them down by either roasting in the oven or on the stovetop with some sugar will get rid of the extra liquid and intensify the strawberry flavour. Freeze dried strawberry powder will also help the flavour be more discernibly strawberry-ish and the cake more naturally pink - but more muted than if you use gel food colouring. The powder can also go into the frosting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I typically don't use a spray on my cake pan but since that was an option I figured I would try. I do not recommend. Take the time to butter and flour your pans. Also if you do use the spray to prep your pans: when you are trimming the cake tops also trim your edges. My cakes were significantly harder on the edges than any other time I make cakes and grease and flour my pans. With how soft this frosting is - it's a nightmare to cut with a harder edge to the cake.

commentBody: I didn’t have gel food coloring so just used a little of the liquid stuff. It was a two day project as I made the cakes one day and then put it together the next. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe, adjusted sugar as advised, and the cake was super dense and heavy in weight. I used 4 8-inch pans instead of the 3 9-inch pans so started checking at around 35min for doneness and the knife was super dry already so i removed from oven. Not eatable - super disappointed - and this was for a large party. I’m going to retry it in a 9-inch pan and leave for 40-45min hoping it’ll be successful. Lesson learned: don’t try new cakes for large parties, remain with what you know !

commentBody: i disagree with the idea of adding wet and dry in three additions each. start with the dry because mixing the flour in first coats the flour and minimizes gluten stimulation. then add half the wet, another third of the dry, then the remaining wet and finally the last of the dry to take up any remaining wetness in the batter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
First had this cake in Midtown Memphis 20 years ago. Then learned to make it at home. My recipe replaces the strawberry preserves with a package of strawberry jello. Yeah, jello, the sugar powder stuff that that comes in a boxed bag.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One way to roast strawberries is this method. I use it all the time with great results! Wash berries and cut only the large ones in half. Gently toss in a bowl with some sugar, juice of half a lemon, and a dash of almond extract. Be careful not to overdo any of the add ins. Place strawberries on a jelly roll pan and roast at 400 for between 30-40 minutes. If strawberries are placed in 1 layer, they won’t produce as much juice. Alternatively, you can use a small Dutch oven and roast for 40 min

commentBody:
Love this! Lemon bars are usually too sweet for me, so I use only 1 C sugar and no powdered sugar.

To prevent seepage, make sure your shortbread is pressed tightly against the pan sides. When it comes out of the oven, if it’s pulled away from the sides, use the back of a spoon to press and re-seal. When adding the curd, pour it over a spatula to prevent making divots (seepage!) in the shortbread.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Big smiles reading the article and recipe! My mother called the dessert "Chocolate Torte". Years ago, I developed basically the same 'from scratch' dessert using organic ingredients. We call it 'Supreme Organic Junk Food' and it is as beloved in my family as the original junk food recipe. Side by side, the scratch version is 100% tastier. If chocolate isn't your thing, a butterscotch version is as easy and delicious. I hope everyone who fondly remembers the dessert will try this version.

commentBody: I completely understand the desire to make a "real" version of this and ditching the store-bought convenience versions of the layers. I hate to think I am making a pitch to use something like Cool Whip, but maybe a stabilizer?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is my all-time favorite dessert, I mean all-time.  I had this for every birthday party in elementary school instead of birthday cake.  This is the fancy version, which is fantastic, but you can certainly include or omit convenience ingredients.  The original recipe uses Jell-o instant pudding and Cool Whip; I don't have a problem with the Jell-o-it's just sugar and cornstarch which go into pudding anyway-but Cool Whip is icky.  Use real whipped cream.  Thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a delicious make ahead dessert that travels well. People commented about using real whipped cream and leftovers being runny. I have had this leftover in the fridge for a little more than 24 hours and there is nothing runny. I used real whipped cream. Don’t whimp out and use graham crackers instead of pecans in the crust. The pecans add an almost salty caramel flavor and some needed texture in an otherwise completely creamy dessert. The results are worth the time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom made a family version of this recipe called “Dessert Secret.” The crust was graham crackers and saltines crushed with butter, topped with Jell-O butterscotch pudding and butter pecan ice cream mixed together, a layer of cool whip, and finally a generous helping of chopped pecans. I grew up in the desert and remember standing in front of the refrigerator in the height of summer, heaping spoonfuls of it into my mouth while the cool air blasted me in the face.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Back in the 70's and 80's in Austin Texas, there was Mike and Charlie's restaurant. "Babe's Delight" was the featured dessert. I remember the shortbread cookie layer, the cream cheese layer (tinted a soft pink), the dark chocolate layer, and the pillowy whipped cream topped with a sprinkle of toasted Texas pecans. You always knew when someone was having it for dessert  when they took the first bite: head tilts back eyes closed, huge closed mouth smile, look of utter "delight!"
Great memory!

Great memory!

commentBody:
BEST METHOD FOR ROASTED PECANS:

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It’s great so many people have memories and various versions of this. I personally grew up eating the boxed chocolate Jello pudding and Cool whip version and always loved it, so I’m partial to this combo of flavors and this recipe does not disappoint at all! While nostalgia has its place, I am much more interested in making something from scratch these days, which might be why I haven’t made this dessert from my childhood. Huge thanks to Lisa for a beautiful recipe! I love the pecans (as opposed to almonds I had experienced in the past), and I will absolutely never use cool whip when this whipped cream was so easy and delicious. I did not have any issue with it holding up in the fridge. Spending a little extra time making it made it all that much better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One of my favorite things to make when I was a kid. The title on my handwritten recipe card is The Next Best Thing to Robert Redford. My crust was made with flour, butter and pecans. My family liked it better than the all chocolate version. To make it fancy, I would grate or shave chocolate on top. I need to make this soon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use a little gelatin to stabilize whipped cream (from The New All Purpose Joy of Cooking). Sprinkle 1/2 tsp. gelatin over 1 Tbsp. cold water in a heatproof custard cup. Let stand 5 minutes to soften. Set custard cup with gelatin in a small pan with a little hot water; heat until gelatin dissolves. Beat 1 cup minus 2 Tbsp. heavy cream, with 1 Tbsp. sugar & 1/2 tsp. vanilla using electric mixer. Stir reserved 2 Tbsp. heavy cream into gelatin mixture & continue beating until stiff enough. Chill.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe looks a lot like a dessert my mother used to make 50 years ago, and which my cousins still rave about. It had a walnut crust, a layer of chocolate pudding, and was made with Cool Whip.  She also had another dessert, equally famous, with yellow cake, vanilla pudding, canned crushed pineapple, Cool Whip, and walnuts.  I've never found the recipes, but I suspect they both came from the inside of a Cool Whip lid.  It would be heresy to say it out loud, but this recipe looks better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As many people have noted, this is the more labor-intensive version of a popular dessert made with Cool Whip and jello pudding called Chocolate Delight (and other names).  The point being, you can sub in any pudding mix (lemon, butterscotch, vanilla, pistachio). You can also swap out the pecans for pulverized oreos, shortbread cookies, pretzels, graham crackers, peanuts etc. If you aren't eating it all in one sitting, the Cool Whip layer lasts longer than homemade whipped cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I had a problem with the salt instructions.  In each part of the recipe that calls for salt, it says to use half as much if using coarser salt.  That doesn't make sense to me, because if you use the same volume of coarse and fine salt, the coarser salt will be less in mass.  So why use half the volume when it's already less?
In the crust, I would use finer salt anyway, because the crystals will stay the same size as when put in, since there is not enough liquid to dissolve them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fell in love with this recipe for its aromatics and versatility. But, encountered 2 problems: the deflation of my egg whites after adding the citrus sugar and the uneven lining of the molds. Both issues arose due to the moisture in the citrus sugar. When I make these again, I will use dehydrated citrus peel in the sugar to line the molds. And, I will use plain sugar in the egg whites. I will use all of the citrus sugar in the custard-base mixture, which can accommodate the moisture.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a myth re being unable to have grapefruit/juice while on statin. A serving of GF now and then (minimal here) is nothing to think about at all. If you love GF daily, as my spouse does, it will likely reduce dose of statin you need as GF results in an more drug in your system. For daily GF lovers, have your liver and lipids checked in 3-6 wks following regular grapefruit ingestion, until you demonstrate stable results on any dose changes indicated. Less drug…YAY!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this using good quality puff pastry. Followed recipe but should have only baked at 400 for 20 minutes - it was dark brown, bordering on burnt, at given temps and timing.  Otherwise good.

This is not something to cook when you are hungry, with the amount of time needed for chilling, freezering and cooling. Also I would use a bit more pimento and less salt.

Otherwise excellent and will make again!

commentBody: Made this for a party and it disappeared. Personally found it too salty and would omit the salt from the cheese pimento mix next time. The crust method is worth following but not so different from my usual way of using a large bowl, shmooshing the butter cubes into the flour with fingers, and a quick knead in the same bowl, thus avoiding a big counter cleanup. The baking pan was too large for the freezer so I put it in the refrigerator for an hour or two before baking which seemed to work fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am a Louisiana native. I've made and eaten pralines (pecan candy) all my life. The first thing about this recipe that I find odd is that it takes 8 hours and more. If your candy takes that long to set, you've not cooked it long enough, 30 minutes or less. The tip about using salt to cut some of the acute sweetness is a good one. But what are pralines if not the holy grail of sweet. Last, we use a can of sweetened condensed milk instead of cream. That cream is why it takes 8 hours--too wet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I lived in New Orleans for 47 years and the only pralines I make are in the microwave. They are delicious and disappear immediately after cooking. My daughter in law made 400 for favors at her wedding. Just cook 1 box of light brown sugar and 8 ounces of whipping cream for 11 minutes on high in the microwave. Add one stick of butter, 1 teaspoon of vanilla flavoring, and chopped pecans, whatever amount you want. Pour out on wax paper on your granite or marble counter. Let cool for hours.

commentBody:

Mixing the caramel makes a much softer, melt-in-your-mouth texture. Decadent.

commentBody: I made a nut free version of these using broken up pretzels, the waffle shaped butter snaps from Snyders. When in doubt use the bigger pan.

commentBody: I've made this recipe multiple times - tweaking it each time. Be careful of the salt. Reduce it if you don't want them to taste quite salty. The baking soda isn't necessary. Watch for the color you want and take off the heat when you get it, once you've reached 240.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Whenever a recipe calls for citrus zest and sugar, I peel the zest with a very sharp vegetable peeler, add the zest along with the sugar in the recipe into a food processor; a few pulses later you have full flavored orange or lemon sugar. For this recipe, you could then add the vanilla, fennel seeds and salt and just pulse a couple more times. Transfer sugar mixture to the bowl of a mixer, and proceed with step 4. Then make the orange puree in the orange scented food processor.  Easy!

commentBody:
Step 2 says to zest one of the oranges to add to the sugar, so that one will not have any peel on it.  The other will.  So I could wash both oranges well before using.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When I make the whole lemon tart on this site, if the lemon has a thick pith, I carefully zest then sloppily peel most but not all of the pith off (discard excess pith), remove the seeds. If a lemon has thin pith, I just seed it and go with the recipe. When I’ve ignored this and chucked in the whole lemons with thick pith and seeds, it is inedible. Keep in mind that the sugar in the recipe is expecting to counter some of the bitterness from the pith. I’d imagine similar rules here.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: About making the orange purée for this cake: cut the ends and discard. Using a potato peeler, peel the orange skin and set aside. Now you will have a naked orange with the white pith on top. Using a sharp knife, remove the pith and reveal the orange. Discard the pith. Cut the orange in quarters and remove all the seeds to discard them. Now you have the orange peel along with tbd orange flesh. Purée these two things for your batter. The whole operation takes a minute or two but well worth it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I read the instruction to 'paddle all the livelong day' and it made me wonder. Usually, one does not beat cake batters all the livelong day because that develops gluten and makes for a tough cake.

I also wondered about the variability of oranges: some have very little pith and some have a thick layer.  Which oranges were used?  

This recipe could use a bit more calibration in order to be friendly for all bakers.  (Disclaimer:  I didn't make this because I had questions.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hot tip for leftovers if you have any.  This cake is delicious but in a small household like mine, I end up with a stale bit leftover a few days in.  I found a use for it that’s very satisfying- croutons!   I cut the stale cake into small cubes and dried them in a cookie sheet in the oven.  An arugula salad with blood orange, celery, blot peppers, cinnamon pecans, and a fig balsamic topped with croutons made from this cake was DELICIOUS.

commentBody:

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As Su notes, stand mixers are seldom necessary for any cake batter, including the creaming of butter and sugar. Spoons, sturdy ones, work very well, and another excellent tool for mixing batters is the Danish dough whisk (q.v. on Amazon to see what they are).  It's faster than a stand mixer and provides more control, and clean up is as easy as washing a spoon. Smaller ones are good for liquid batters such as pancakes, larger ones for stiffer doughs and mixing ground meat with other ingredients.

commentBody: I thought this recipe sounded divine, so tried it for the first time.  Followed the recipe & placed the cast iron in oven at least 1/2hr before the baking of the cake. The middle wouldn't bake completely - I ended up taking out of the oven after 1hr of baking. The edges were brown/delish - middle undercooked.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this yesterday. In the article she says any nut flour will do so I used almond flour in place of the semolina. I have a well seasoned 8” iron skillet that I used. Everything else I did exactly as the recipe is written. The resulting cake was wonderfully fragrant, nutty and rustic. But the middle was underdone, probably because the skillet was too small. I also didn’t like the slight bitterness from the orange pith. Next time I’ll cut the recipe in half and trim more of the pith.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Navels have few to no seeds and a minimum of pith. Mine were large enough to yield 2 cups of puree. Followed the advice of another commenter and pulsed the zest, fennel, vanilla, pinch of salt and sugar (<350 grams) in a food processor first. Loved the toasted fennel and walnuts. Came out perfectly after 45 minutes. Moist and tender and flavorful. My husband loves the chantilly cream but the cake is delicious on its own without the embellishment.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Since our backyard oranges are small and less sweet, I ended up rough cutting off about half the skins of 4 and all the skins of 2 to reach the 1 ½ cups of puree. 
I wanted to make sure the cake was moist and so I instead used ½ cup of oil with ½ cup of butter.  Also, I used an instant thermometer and pulled it at 205 degrees to avoid over baking
The topping was too subtle for me, not sweet enough and seemed too light bodied so I added more sugar and a little cream cheese for body.
So Good!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake yesterday. I  had some doubts about using the entire orange, pith, skin, and all, but I followed the recipe as written, zesting one orange into the sugar and then cutting it and the second orange in quarters to puree. It is fantastic! I did use a 12" cast iron skillet because that's what I have, and it worked fine. Using the entire orange results in an intensely orange, slightly bitter puree, but the bitterness is lost when the cake comes together. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No fennel or semolina, but everything else as written. Texture is dense and moist, in a positive way. The citrus: as is this cake was bitter, although not so much that it was inedible, but it is a prominent note. Only removed the stem end as directed and the oranges I had did contain a fair amount of pith. I could see zesting both, and letting the osmotic pressure of the sugar draw the oil out like an oleo saccharum, followed by removing a majority of the pith. Will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We’ve made this several times, and have loved it. But: there is a reason, we think, for the advice to serve/enjoy warm right out of the oven. As the cake cools, the middle (the dome) will start to sink. So if made ahead for guests, it will look like a bit of a collapsed cake, even though it is thoroughly cooked throughout. We just tried baking this in a springform pan. The batter filled up the pan much higher, so we baked it much longer (60 min). It still sank, even more, in the middle over the course of 4 hrs. We’ve concluded (though we should’ve recognized this from the start) that this is a very heavy batter. Right out of the oven, there is still enough warmth to keep that middle from sinking. Our plan: go back to cast iron pan, and don’t bake hours in advance for guests. Easy enough to do the prep ahead and bake later.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Note to the food police: You can't arbitrarily reduce the amount of sugar in a cake recipe without adversely affecting flavor and texture. This is a "high ratio" cake in which the amount of sugar exceeds the amount of flour. The sugar's needed to offset the bitterness of the cocoa, which gives the cake its rich chocolate flavor. If the cake offends your sensibilities, have a piece of fruit for dessert instead.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A kind note to those with health concerns. I participate in a wild game cooking FB group with a few simple rules. The most important rule is you never criticize what someone else eats.  Everyone is different.  Some want a cake with all the sugar and all the butter. It's cake! There is nothing inherently wrong with that.  If it appeals to you, make it and tell us how it was.  If it doesn't, just move on to something else.  Why shame others for liking what they like?  Me?  I'm making the cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Back in the days when I had reasons to bake cakes, I found that baking the layers in advance led to the best results. Bake them, cool completely (out of the pan) and refrigerate wrapped in plastic. As for making this recipe my own, I'd use strong hot coffee instead of the water and raspberry preserves between the layers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hey cake chefs.  there are plenty of cake recipes that used less sugar and more flour.  These are just different cakes. So, if that is your need use a different recipe to create your layers.  one thing this type of recipe does for the chef is less mounding of the cake as it bakes.  Cakes with sour creme, yogurt or creme as an ingredient general have this attribute which lends itself well to multi-layers + higher sugar content with the creme makes the layers easier to handle in my experience.

commentBody: I’m a true Southern (Alabama). I made this cake to take to Christmas dinner at a friend’s house out of town, so I made it a few days in advance. I followed this recipe exactly as written, weighing every ingredient as I went. I refrigerated after frosting each layer. Once finished, I put the cake in a sealed cake carrier and stored it in our 50 degree garage (we live in New England now(. It was absolutely perfect! I would not change a thing! Huge hit a dinner!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Average. The frosting is dairy-heavy and a bit sweet, not lusciously chocolatey (I used good chocolate and organic whipping cream). The cake is a decent oil cake, moist and reasonably flavorful and easy to make and so a good general purpose recipe, but the contrast to the frosting isn't marked or interesting. A better frosting would make a big difference. 

The cake is also huge, hard to get in the fridge. Not the right amount for a household of two without reducing amounts and pan size.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A lovely, rich and beautiful cake, simple to make and easy to frost.  Refrigeration between steps was key for me. Added strawberry jam (more sugar!) between layers.  Had lots of frosting leftover. Will try a peanut butter option between layers next time.  Was forced to leave cakes in their pans overnight for transport, which left the finished product so moist it compressed beneath the slicing knife.  Will bake ahead next time again, but will overnight out of the pans!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is very similar to a Texas Chocolate Sheet (Sheath) Cake - except that it is much larger and in the form of a layer cake, vs. a sheet pan cake. It was super easy to make and was gorgeous. I found eating one piece, as small as I could cut it, was difficult because it is so rich. Make it for a crowd! You can also try cutting it like a wedding cake, where you cut a circle about 3" into the cake - cut slices from that; then move into cutting the center circle.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this recipe a few times now using the below modifications with great results.
Replace hot water with strong black hot coffee, used PAM Canola Oil Baking Spray with Flour,  replaced flour with Cup-For-Cup Gluten Free Flour for a family member with allergies and cake came out the same as its regular flour counterparts (texture, taste, shelf-life, bake times). Used vanilla buttercream frosting in place of chocolate frosting (also adding almond and butter extract to recipe with great fanfare)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this several times now and it's always a hit. Incredibly moist cake, tasty frosting, overall a crowd pleaser for chocolate lovers! 

I have halved the recipe and baked in 3, 6" layers which works perfectly. Only tricky part is the eggs, but I crack two eggs and pour a little out. I've also used semisweet instead of unsweetened chocolate for the frosting since that's what I usually have handy, but cut down on the a sugar. 

To get a lighter colored frosting like the photo, you do need to whip some air into the ganache - I prefer it this way from a texture and visual perspective. Just whipped it in my stand mixer for a minute or two and popped it in the fridge to get the consistency and color I want. 

The layers will be extremely moist, so make sure they are completely cooled before assembling.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First-time cake baker here...followed the recipe to the "T" but somehow missed the subtle (to me) instructions to "gently whisk" the batter. Not having made such a cake before, and a bit overwhelmed by the large volume of batter, I whisked away with gusto. Result? Overall texture seems a bit "brownie" to me, perhaps chewy rather than the "velvety" I hear from other commenters. Googling a bit turns up talk of "rubbery" cakes of which I think mine is a relative. Oh well, on to the next one!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We made this triple layer cake today, the third layer cake recipe we’ve followed from The NY Times. This cake, like the others (with quite different recipes) has risen nicely in the oven. But all three cakes have sunk down quite flat as they cool. I’m sure the flavor will be great, but without them rising we can’t get any sort of impressive height! We have this problem regardless of whichever layer cake recipe we follow (and we follow them precisely!). What are we doing wrong?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake yesterday for Father’s Day and my family loved it! My daughter even commented on how it looked like a cake from a local bakery! A few notes, my frosting was definitely thin, I added about a half cup of heavy cream, a little confectionery sugar and used a hand beater. I frosted most of it, chilled the cake and the remaining frosting  I made and iced the rest of it afterwards. Next time I’ll chill the frosting after it reaches room temp. I used hot coffee as well in the batter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This a marvelously chocolatey cake.  It’s simple to make, no mixer required.  I used leftover boiling coffee instead of boiling water.  I omitted the vanilla.  (Why is it even there?  Habit leftover from when it was much less expensive, I suspect.) I decreased the sugar by 100 grams, or so, and nobody complained. Nay, they couldn’t stop talking about how good it was.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this in an 11 x 15 glass pan for a group. I used 6 ounces less sugar. The cake was so moist and perfect. Don’t overcook it! For me even though it hadn’t pulled away from the edges, it popped back when the middle was pressed.  I did not have whipping cream for the frosting so I made simple cooked fudge frosting. This fool proof recipe is done by heating 1 cup sugar, 1/3 cup milk and 5 T butter over medium heat stirring often in a sauce pan to boiling point. Boil one minute and remove from the heat. Add 1 cup chocolate chips (I like extra dark) and stir until melted. Pour this mixture while still hot over the cooled cake. It sets to perfection in a few minutes. I had leftovers and froze them. It has been a tasty treat a month later. This is a great, reliable and very easy recipe.
